SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are supported by experiments: Section 4.2 reports comparisons to state-of-the-art methods and 4.3 shows concrete self-training gains (e.g., 6% on Wikipedia and 12% on Non-Wikipedia for Turkish). The methods (BiLSTM-CRF, NPMI seed selection, self-training, GIZA++ translations, and collective linking using salience/similarity/coherence) are standard and reasonably applied (Sections 2.3, 2.5, 3.3). However, several methodological details are under-specified in the excerpt (choice of τ for NPMI, clustering procedure and n, thresholds for high-confidence selection) and no statistical significance testing is reported, leaving some minor gaps in reproducibility and confidence in marginal improvements. The paper also explicitly notes limitations (e.g., morphology analysis only for concatenative affixation), which helps justify conclusions.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Most core techniques are adaptations of existing work: BiLSTM-CRF name tagging (Lample et al., 2016), NPMI seed selection (Guo et al., 2014), GIZA++ for title-pair translation, and collective linking measures from Pan et al. (2015) (Sections 2.3, 2.5, 3.1, 3.3). The primary novel aspects appear to be the scale (applying the pipeline to 282 Wikipedia languages) and practical KB-derived morphology/affix mining from anchor links, which is a useful but incremental contribution. Overall the paper integrates known components at large scale rather than introducing fundamentally new algorithms.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into clear sections (overview, name tagging, KB features, linking, experiments) and explicitly references figures/tables (Figure 2, Table 1) that structure the exposition. However, the provided excerpt contains many repeated/parasitic lines and some parameter/notation omissions (e.g., exact definitions of τ, n, confidence thresholds), and several references to figures/tables not present here make parts harder to follow. The main contributions are stated, but certain methodological descriptions would benefit from more precise, compact presentation.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental evaluation is reasonably extensive: results on Wikipedia for 282 languages and Non-Wikipedia (DARPA LORELEI) evaluations for 9 languages are reported (Section 4.2), with ablations showing morphology gains for Turkish and Uzbek and self-training curves for Turkish (Section 4.3). Nevertheless, ablation/sensitivity studies are limited to a few components/languages rather than across the full pipeline, no statistical tests are shown, and some implementation/reproducibility details (hyperparameters, clustering algorithm specifics, data splits) are missing from the excerpt. The related work coverage cites many prior efforts, indicating fair literature grounding.

IMPACT.justification: The work addresses an important practical problem—scalable cross-lingual name tagging and linking across 282 languages—and promises public release of data and systems, which would be widely useful to the community (introduction/goal statements). Demonstrated competitive performance on non-Wikipedia data and gains from low-cost morphology/self-training techniques suggest broad applicability. While not likely to fundamentally change theory, the scale and resource contribution could significantly influence multilingual NLP practice.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (cross-lingual name tagging and entity linking for many languages) is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues and addresses a community-relevant audience (DARPA LORELEI, multilingual NLP researchers). The methods and evaluation (comparison to recent baselines, experiments on standard non-Wikipedia datasets) meet the methodological expectations for such venues (Sections 4.2, related work). The paper is well-positioned for the venue's research community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares to recent language-independent baselines (Zhang et al., 2016a; Tsai et al., 2016) and to supervised models trained on gold data, and it reports absolute F-score differences (Section 4.2). It also cites and builds on many relevant prior linking and tagging methods (numerous references in Sections 2 and 3). However, the excerpt lacks full detail on whether all comparisons used identical data splits, exact baseline configurations, or significance testing, so fairness of some comparisons is not fully verifiable from the provided text.
